questionText: With a $1500\mathrm{M}\Omega$ resistor across its terminals, the terminal voltage of a certain battery is $2.50\mathrm{V}$. With only a $5.00\Omega$ resistor across its terminals, the terminal voltage is $1.75\mathrm{V}$ (a) Find the internal emf and the internal resistance of this battery. (b) What would be the terminal voltage if the $5.00\Omega$ resistor were replaced by a $7.00\Omega$ resistor?

parts.label: a
parts.question: Find the internal emf and the internal resistance of this battery.

parts.label: b
parts.question: What would be the terminal voltage if the $5.00\Omega$ resistor were replaced by a $7.00\Omega$ resistor?

questionText: An automobile starter motor is connected to a $12.0\mathrm{V}$ battery. When the starter is activated it draws $150\mathrm{A}$ of current, and the battery voltage drops to $7.0\mathrm{V}$. What is the battery's internal resistance?

Consider the circuit shown in Figure 19.54. The terminal voltage of the $24.0\mathrm{V}$ battery is $21.2\mathrm{V}$. What is (a) the internal resistance $r$ of the battery; (b) the resistance $R$ of the circuit resistor?

parts.label: a
parts.question: the internal resistance $r$ of the battery

parts.label: b
parts.question: the resistance $R$ of the circuit resistor?

questionText: A resistor with a $15.0\mathrm{V}$ potential difference across its ends develops thermal energy at a rate of 327 W. (a) What is the current in the resistor? (b) What is its resistance?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the current in the resistor?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What is its resistance?

questionText: Power rating of a resistor. The power rating of a resistor is the maximum power it can safely dissipate without being damaged by overheating. (a) If the power rating of a certain $15\mathrm{k}\Omega$ resistor is $5.0\mathrm{W}$, what is the maximum current it can carry without damage? What is the greatest allowable potential difference across the terminals of this resistor? (b) If a $9.0\mathrm{k}\Omega$ resistor is to be connected across a $120\mathrm{V}$ potential difference, what power rating is required for that resistor?

parts.label: a
parts.question: If the power rating of a certain $15\mathrm{k}\Omega$ resistor is $5.0\mathrm{W}$, what is the maximum current it can carry without damage? What is the greatest allowable potential difference across the terminals of this resistor?

parts.label: b
parts.question: If a $9.0\mathrm{k}\Omega$ resistor is to be connected across a $120\mathrm{V}$ potential difference, what power rating is required for that resistor?

questionText: An idealized voltmeter is connected across the terminals of a $15.0\mathrm{V}$ battery, and a $75.0\Omega$ appliance is also connected across its terminals. If the voltmeter reads $11.3\mathrm{V}$: (a) how much power is being dissipated by the appliance, and (b) what is the internal resistance of the battery?

parts.label: a
parts.question: how much power is being dissipated by the appliance

parts.label: b
parts.question: what is the internal resistance of the battery?

questionText: BIO Treatment of heart failure. A heart defibrillator is used to enable the heart to start beating if it has stopped. This is done by passing a large current of 12 A through the body at $25\mathrm{V}$ for a very short time, usually about $3.0~\mathrm{ms}$. (a) What power does the defibrillator deliver to the body, and (b) how much energy is transferred?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What power does the defibrillator deliver to the body

parts.label: b
parts.question: how much energy is transferred?

questionText: Lightbulb. The wattage rating of a lightbulb is the power it consumes when it is connected across a $120\mathrm{V}$ potential difference. For example, a $60\mathrm{W}$ lightbulb consumes $60.0\mathrm{W}$ of electric power only when it is connected across a $120\mathrm{V}$ potential difference. (a) What is the resistance of a $60\mathrm{W}$ lightbulb? (b) Without doing any calculations, would you expect a $100\mathrm{W}$ bulb to have more or less resistance than a $60\mathrm{W}$ bulb? Calculate and find out.

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the resistance of a $60\mathrm{W}$ lightbulb?

parts.label: b
parts.question: Without doing any calculations, would you expect a $100\mathrm{W}$ bulb to have more or less resistance than a $60\mathrm{W}$ bulb? Calculate and find out.

questionText: BIO Electrical safety. This procedure is not recommended! You'll see why after you work the problem. You are on an aluminum ladder that is standing on the ground, trying to fix an electrical connection with a metal screwdriver that has a metal handle. Your body is wet because you are sweating from the exertion; therefore, it has a resistance of $1.0\mathrm{k}\Omega$. (a) If you accidentally touch the "hot" wire connected to the $120\mathrm{V}$ line, how much current will pass through your body? Is this amount enough to be dangerous? (The maximum safe current is about $5\mathrm{mA}$.) (b) How much electric power is delivered to your body?

parts.label: a
parts.question: If you accidentally touch the "hot" wire connected to the $120\mathrm{V}$ line, how much current will pass through your body? Is this amount enough to be dangerous? (The maximum safe current is about $5\mathrm{mA}$.)

parts.label: b
parts.question: How much electric power is delivered to your body?

questionText: BIO Electric eels. Electric eels generate electric pulses along their skin that can be used to stun an enemy when they come into contact with it. Tests have shown that these pulses can be up to $500\mathrm{V}$ and produce currents of $80\mathrm{mA}$ (or even larger). A typical pulse lasts for $10\mathrm{ms}$. What power and how much energy are delivered to the unfortunate enemy with a single pulse, assuming a steady current?

A $500\Omega$ and a $200\Omega$ resistor are connected in series with an ideal battery that has an emf of $20\mathrm{V}$. (a) What current flows through each resistor? (b) What power is delivered to each resistor? (c) What power is supplied by the battery?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What current flows through each resistor?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What power is delivered to each resistor?

parts.label: c
parts.question: What power is supplied by the battery?

questionText: The battery for a certain cell phone is rated at $3.70\mathrm{V}$. According to the manufacturer it can produce $3.15 \times 10^{4}\mathrm{J}$ of electrical energy, enough for $5.25\mathrm{h}$ of operation, before needing to be recharged. Find the average current that this cell phone draws when turned on.

For the circuit in Figure 19.55, find (a) the rate of conversion of internal (chemical) energy to electrical energy within the battery, (b) the rate of dissipation of electrical energy in the battery, (c) the rate of dissipation of electrical energy in the external resistor.

parts.label: a
parts.question: the rate of conversion of internal (chemical) energy to electrical energy within the battery,

parts.label: b
parts.question: the rate of dissipation of electrical energy in the battery,

parts.label: c
parts.question: the rate of dissipation of electrical energy in the external resistor.

questionText: A 240 W electric heater is designed to operate from a $12.0\mathrm{V}$ car battery. (a) What is its resistance? (b) What current does it draw? (c) If the battery voltage drops to $11\mathrm{V}$, what power does the heater take? (Assume that the resistance is constant.)

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is its resistance?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What current does it draw?

parts.label: c
parts.question: If the battery voltage drops to $11\mathrm{V}$, what power does the heater take? (Assume that the resistance is constant.)

questionText: Find the equivalent resistance of each combination shown in Figure 19.56.
Calculate the (a) maximum and (b) minimum values of resistance that can be obtained by combining resistors of $36\Omega$, $47\Omega$, and $51\Omega$.

parts.label: a
parts.question: maximum

parts.label: b
parts.question: minimum values of resistance that can be obtained by combining resistors of $36\Omega$, $47\Omega$, and $51\Omega$.

questionText: Each of two identical uniform metal bars has a resistance $R$. (a) The end of one bar is welded to the end of the other bar. In terms of $R$, what is the resistance of this combination? (b) What is the resistance if the bars are welded together along their length? (See Figure 19.57.)

parts.label: a
parts.question: The end of one bar is welded to the end of the other bar. In terms of $R$, what is the resistance of this combination?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What is the resistance if the bars are welded together along their length?

questionText: A 40.0 Ω resistor and a 90.0 Ω resistor are connected in parallel, and the combination is connected across a 120 V dc line. (a) What is the resistance of the parallel combination? (b) What is the total current through the parallel combination? (c) What is the current through each resistor?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the resistance of the parallel combination?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What is the total current through the parallel combination?

parts.label: c
parts.question: What is the current through each resistor?

questionText: Three resistors having resistances of 1.60 Ω, 2.40 Ω, and 4.80 Ω are connected in parallel to a 28.0 V battery that has negligible internal resistance. Find (a) the equivalent resistance of the combination, (b) the current in each resistor, (c) the total current through the battery, (d) the voltage across each resistor, and (e) the power dissipated in each resistor. (f) Which resistor dissipates the most power, the one with the greatest resistance or the one with the least resistance? Explain why this should be.

parts.label: a
parts.question: the equivalent resistance of the combination,

parts.label: b
parts.question: the current in each resistor,

parts.label: c
parts.question: the total current through the battery,

parts.label: d
parts.question: the voltage across each resistor, and

parts.label: e
parts.question: the power dissipated in each resistor.

parts.label: f
parts.question: Which resistor dissipates the most power, the one with the greatest resistance or the one with the least resistance? Explain why this should be.

questionText: Now the three resistors of the previous problem are connected in series to the same battery. Answer the same questions for this situation.
Compute the equivalent resistance of the network in Figure 19.58, and find the current in each resistor. The battery has negligible internal resistance.
Compute the equivalent resistance of the network in Figure 19.59, and find the current in each resistor. The battery has negligible internal resistance.

I Lightbulbs in series. The power rating of a lightbulb is the power it consumes when connected across a 120 V outlet. (a) If you put two 100 W bulbs in series across a 120 V outlet, how much power would each consume if its resistance were constant? (b) How much power does each one consume if you connect them in parallel across a 120 V outlet?

parts.label: a
parts.question: If you put two 100 W bulbs in series across a 120 V outlet, how much power would each consume if its resistance were constant?

parts.label: b
parts.question: How much power does each one consume if you connect them in parallel across a 120 V outlet?

questionText: You have two 10 Ω resistors and one 40 Ω resistor. What combination of these will produce an equivalent resistance of (a) 45 Ω, (b) 18 Ω, (c) 4.44 Ω?
The batteries shown in the circuit in Figure 19.60 have negligibly small internal resistances. Find the current through (a) the 30.0 Ω resistor, (b) the 20.0 Ω resistor, and (c) the 10.0 V battery.

parts.label: a
parts.question: the 30.0 Ω resistor,

parts.label: b
parts.question: the 20.0 Ω resistor, and

parts.label: c
